hype.text: NATO just put Palantir's Maven Smart System at the heart of Allied Command Operations -- 20,000+ users across 35 military tools, with the DoD ceiling lifted to $1.3 billion and a program-of-record designation now locked. The Army went bigger: a $10B enterprise agreement consolidating Palantir software across the service. Anduril's Lattice landed an $86M SOCOM autonomy contract and a parallel $20B+ Army integration deal. General Atomics' CCA drone wingman is back in the air after April's crash. Lockheed broke ground on a THAAD plant that quadruples interceptor production. F-35Bs flew first tests with British SPEAR cruise missiles. Trump deployed 5,000 troops to Poland. Google DeepMind shipped Antigravity; OpenAI quietly bought Tomoro. The pattern: AI-enabled targeting moving from prototype to program-of-record, with the industrial base finally tooling up to feed it.

briefing_narrative.text: The corpus's 426 documents and 370 typed relationships concentrate on three structural shifts. First, software-enabled targeting consolidates around Palantir's Maven Smart System -- NATO acquisition, a $1.3 billion DoD ceiling, and a $10 billion Army enterprise agreement signal program-of-record permanence rather than experimentation. Second, autonomy contracts move to multi-billion enterprise vehicles: SOCOM's $86M Mission Autonomy deal with Anduril and the Army's parallel $20B+ Lattice agreement consolidate what were 120+ separate procurements. Third, the industrial base is rebuilding: Lockheed breaks ground on a 4x THAAD production line, IAI unveils Diamond containerized naval munitions, General Atomics returns its CCA wingman to flight after the April crash, and Navy advances seven MUSV prototypes. Trending AI topics -- reinforcement learning, electronic warfare, collaborative combat aircraft, Golden Dome -- all point at fielded systems rather than capability demos. Key participants this week: Palantir, Anduril, NATO, the U.S. Army, U.S. Navy, Lockheed Martin, Google DeepMind, and OpenAI.
